kept.text:
A moat is what separates a good business from a great one.

It's the competitive advantage that keeps competitors out and profits in.

Here are the 6 types of moats — with concrete examples.

1. SWITCHING COSTS

TurboTax by Intuit.

In the US, filing taxes is a nightmare.

Intuit's products are so widespread that TurboTax = tax season.

The psychological toll of switching is too high.

You simply won't do it.

Your data is in their system. Your history is there. Starting over with a competitor? Too painful.

That's a moat.

2. INTANGIBLE ASSETS

This includes patents and brands.

A red can in a restaurant? Coca-Cola.

You don't even need to see the logo.

Example for patents: Texas Instruments holds ~50,000 patents.

Good luck competing with that.

3. NETWORK EFFECTS

Mastercard & Visa.

The value of the product grows as more people use it.

There's no way around them.

Or put differently: As a restaurant, you'll have a problem if you don't accept Visa/Mastercard.

The more merchants accept them, the more consumers want them.

The more consumers use them, the more merchants must accept them.

Self-reinforcing loop.

4. SIZE AND COST ADVANTAGE

Walmart is the largest retailer in the world.

Its enormous size gives it significant negotiating power.

Suppliers need Walmart more than Walmart needs any single supplier.

That pricing power flows straight to the bottom line.

5. ECONOMIES OF SCALE

In a market of limited size, potential new competitors have little incentive to enter.

Waste Management ($WM) is the largest waste disposal company in the US.

If 7 out of 8 neighbors already have the known, trusted waste company under contract — you're not entering that market.

The scale player wins by default.

6. SOFT MOATS

This is the hardest to quantify.

It could be Alphabet's company culture.

Or Danaher's long-tenured management team and the famous Danaher Business System (Kaizen method).

Soft moats are real — but harder to measure.

THE TAKEAWAY

A first-class business model — a "Good Story" — begins with one of these moats.

It creates a strong foundation for everything that follows.

And companies with durable competitive advantages are often found where monopolies, duopolies, or oligopolies exist.

Moats matter.

Because without a moat, competitors eat your lunch.

With a moat, you eat theirs.

kept.text:
🇺🇦✈️💥 𝗥𝘂𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗮'𝘀 𝗦𝗔𝗠 𝗨𝗺𝗯𝗿𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗮 𝗶𝘀 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗮𝗽𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿 𝗦𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗱 𝗨𝗸𝗿𝗮𝗶𝗻𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝗗𝗿𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗔𝘁𝘁𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻

Ukraine's long-range and intermediate drone operations have transitioned from tactical disruption to a systematic campaign of strategic attrition against Russian air defenses. 

Russian SAM systems and associated radars are being destroyed at a rate that exceeds replenishment capacity, while parallel strikes degrade the production base.

This represents a clear shift in the air war dynamics driven by Ukraine’s scaled drone production and targeting discipline.

𝗣𝗿𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗗𝗘𝗔𝗗 𝗢𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀

Ukrainian forces employ a multi-layered targeting process for Destruction of Enemy Air Defenses (DEAD). 

Reconnaissance drones provide initial location data, while special reconnaissance teams operating forward of the line of contact, combined with ELINT, SIGINT, MASINT, and OSINT, refine targeting on SAM launchers, radars, and support nodes. 

Strike drones equipped with 60-160 kg warheads execute the terminal engagement. 

Follow-on reconnaissance drones conduct battle damage assessment.

Unmanned Systems Forces (USF) units have averaged 40+ confirmed air defense component destructions per month in early 2026, with approximately two-thirds targeting radar systems. 

Special reconnaissance teams supply critical ground-level details on system movements, logistics, and emission patterns that overhead and satellite collection cannot fully resolve. 

Multi-source intelligence fusion accelerates the cycle from detection to strike; often critical for real-time targeting of highly mobile SAM systems.

𝗦𝗰𝗮𝗹𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗥𝘂𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝗟𝗼𝘀𝘀𝗲𝘀

The SBU's Alpha unit has played a prominent role in deep strikes. 

It assessed that it neutralized roughly half of Russia's operational Pantsir inventory across 2025 and early 2026, each system valued at $15-20M. 

Alpha has also conducted multiple operations against S-300/350/400 complexes, destroying launchers and high-end radars including 92N6E "Grave Stone" units, valued at $30-60M per unit. 

The unit reported approximately $4 billion in total Russian air defense and radar losses for 2025 alone.

A single FP-2 strike drone costs around $50,000.

𝗗𝗿𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗟𝗮𝘂𝗻𝗰𝗵 𝗩𝗼𝗹𝘂𝗺𝗲

In March 2026, Ukrainian long-range drone launches reached parity with Russia's for the first time in the conflict and exceeded them on multiple nights. Primary platforms include:

• Liutyi (AN-196 "Fierce") | Type - Long-range one-way attack drone, fixed-wing, propeller-driven | Range: 1,000-2,000 km | Warhead: 50-75 kg HE-fragmentation | Mass: 250-300 kg | Role: Deep strikes on strategic rear targets | Cost: ~$200,000

• FP-1 (Fire Point-1) | Type- Long-range one-way attack drone, plywood/composite, propeller-driven | Range: up to ~1,600 km | Warhead: 50-120 kg modular | Role: Deep strikes on logistics, command posts, air defenses, and infrastructure | Cost: ~$55,000

• FP-2 (Fire Point-2) | Type - Intermediate-range strike drone, heavy payload focus | Range: ~200 km | FPV capable terminal guidance | Warhead: 60-158 kg, baseline 105-120 kg, adaptable for aviation munitions | Role - Operations against SAM launchers and radars, naval vessels, stationary aircraft, EW, C2, Comms nodes, high-value fixed infrastructure | Cost: ~$50,000

𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗸𝗲𝘀 𝗼𝗻 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗜𝗻𝗳𝗿𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲

Ukrainian forces have simultaneously targeted the Russian military-industrial base including:

• Votkinsk plant (February 2026): Iskander and Kinzhal missile components.

• Kremniy El facility (March 2026): microchips and control systems for S-300, S-400, Pantsir, and Buk systems.

• Energia factory (January 2026, with prior strikes in 2025): battery and power source components for missiles and drones.

• Kupol Electromechanical Plant (July 2025): Tor and Osa SAM systems (plus related drones). 

Russia's production of long-range SAM batteries is limited to a handful per year under normal conditions.

𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗰 𝗗𝗲𝗹𝘁𝗮

Monthly attrition rates of 40+ air defense components, combined with direct strikes on production nodes, are opening coverage gaps faster than Russian industry can close them at current output levels. 

The result is more strike packages reaching their targets, more confirmed kills, and more degradation of Russia’s military and economic capacity.

Source: USF | SBU | Defence-UA | Militarnyi | Kyiv Post | Open Source Imagery

kept.text:
The simple graphics of a classic RPG add a layer of abstraction. You're meant to intuit, for example, that you're only seeing a slice of the game world; the "real" game world is bigger. Guardia Kingdom obviously has more than the ~50 inhabitants you see. You figure out that battles, where characters spend most of their time standing still, are just a representation of a more dynamic process that's slowed down and abstracted away for the player's benefit.

"Remakes" of classic pixel RPGs remove this layer of abstraction; they weaken the player's suspension of disbelief, and the aesthetics of the game suffer. And usually, the aesthetics of the original game were fine. If anything, the "problem" is that by modern standards, the game isn't big or complex enough (too short and repetitive, battle programming is too simple, game mechanics make little sense and/or are too easy to optimize, and in general there aren't enough difficult decisions to make).

Unfortunately, this shortcoming of the original 16-bit game usually isn't what gets updated. Square doesn't release a version of Final Fantasy 6 or Chrono Trigger with better battle mechanics, more differentiated characters, etc. They release the game with updated (read: usually worse) graphics and sound and almost no change to the actual gameplay other than maybe the addition of an optional side dungeon.

kept.text:
𝗦𝗛𝗔𝗚𝗢𝗟 𝗔𝗜𝗥𝗙𝗜𝗘𝗟𝗗: 𝗗𝗘𝗘𝗣𝗘𝗦𝗧 𝗦𝗧𝗥𝗜𝗞𝗘 𝗢𝗡 𝗦𝗨-𝟱𝟳𝘀 𝗧𝗢 𝗗𝗔𝗧𝗘

BLUF: Ukrainian Unmanned Systems Forces struck Shagol airfield on April 25, 1,700 km from the Ukrainian border, hitting two Su-57s, one Su-34, and one unidentified Sukhoi variant. Satellite imagery confirms the hits. Russia has not commented.

Shagol sits in Chelyabinsk Oblast, east of the Urals.

The executing unit was the 1st Separate Unmanned Systems Centre, Magyar's Birds. Commander Robert Brovdi confirmed four aircraft hit, with a service vehicle destroyed between the Su-57s. 

Post-strike satellite reconnaissance (Exilenova+, April 17 vs. April 26) shows blast marks, debris, and relocation of affected aircraft to restricted sections of the airfield, consistent with damage assessment procedure.

Damage assessment is ongoing. The Su-34 runs $35-50M per airframe. 

The Su-57 runs $100-120M. Russia's operational Su-57 fleet is estimated at fewer than 30 airframes total.

On August 1, 2025, Lieutenant General Alexander Maxintsev, First Deputy Commander-in-Chief of the Russian Aerospace Forces, stated publicly that protective shelters would be installed at all Russian military airfields. 

Nine months later, Shagol had none.

Russian military analyst Romanov noted the gap, then pondered whether the shelter funds may have found their way to a luxury apartment at a Kremlin relative's address.

Brovdi's closing statement on the operation: "More will undoubtedly follow."

#OSINT #Ukraine
